text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the pars flaccida of the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the right cruce.
The surgeon retracts the fat adjacent to the crura, splaying out the peritoneal covering to enhance visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to score down to the muscle fibers, aiming to reach the correct plane for dissecting the hernia sac away from the mediastinal structures.
The surgeon leaves the anterior covering intact on the cruce, maintaining the tissue layer over the structure.
The surgeon spreads the tissue around the exposed cruce, achieving greater dissection with each movement due to the full exposure of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues dissection over the anterior esophagus, utilizing an avascular plane to separate the tissue without causing bleeding.
The surgeon carefully identifies the anterior vagus nerve at the upper apex of the cruce before proceeding to ligate the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon scores the left cruce by turning the wrist of the instrument downward, carefully opening the peritoneum overlying the cura.
The surgeon spreads the tissue in the same avascular plane previously dissected from the opposite side, maintaining exposure of the cruce.
The surgeon identifies the avascular plane and continues dissection by spreading within this plane on the anterior and right side of the esophagus, enhancing exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to extend into the avascular plane around the anterior esophagus, employing a spreading motion to effectively open up the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies a vessel and uses a spreading technique on either side before dividing the vessel.
The surgeon carefully divides the tissue plane on the right side, ensuring the vagus nerve is not visible within the tissue before proceeding with the division.
The anterior vagus nerve is visible below the surgical instrument, appearing intact and well preserved during the dissection.
The surgeon shifts focus to the posterior window, preparing to dissect and expose anatomical structures in this area.

The surgeon leaves the peritoneal covering intact at the confluence of the crus and gently spreads the tissue to reveal the posterior window. A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus to assist with retraction.
The surgeon focuses on the posterior dissection, carefully manipulating instruments to separate tissue layers and expose anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects over the aorta, a challenging area that requires precise technique to avoid complications.
The robotic camera provides a clear view of the tissue within the dissected plane, highlighting the precision and visibility achieved during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully identifies the path of the posterior vagus nerve, ensuring its location is clear before proceeding with division.
The surgeon uses their left hand to lift the esophagus while preparing to divide tissue with their right hand.
The robotic-assisted view provides a clear visualization of the vessels that need to be divided, as well as the distinct plane between the esophagus and the aorta.
The surgeon completes the high dissection of the esophagus, finalizing its mobilization within the mediastinum using robotic visualization.
The surgeon demonstrates two to three centimeters of intra-abdominal esophageal length and begins closing the crura using a V-lock suture.
The surgeon brings the crura together smoothly, running a V-lock suture evenly spaced towards the esophagus and the posterior space, ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon uses an Ethabon suture to close the crura, demonstrating the flexibility in suture choice for this step of the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the appropriate tension and closure of the esophagus at rest, following the completion of the crura closure with a V-lock suture.
The surgeon runs the V-lock suture back on itself to secure the closure of the crura, ensuring the suture is locked into place.
The surgeon places a mesh over the crural closure area during the parasophageal hernia repair, preparing for fundoplication to support the closure and reduce potential recurrences.
The surgeon positions a Phasix ST mesh over the hernia site and begins suturing it in place using a Vicryl suture.
The surgeon retracts the tissue adjacent to the crura, spreading out the peritoneal covering to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon scores the peritoneal covering down to the crural muscle, then proceeds to open the crura along its entire length, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon preserves the anterior covering of the cruse while carefully opening the space around it, maintaining the integrity of the tissue during the dissection.
The surgeon holds the hernia sac in the right hand while carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue outside of the hernia sac.
The surgeon gently spreads the avascular planes to dissect the hernia sac out of the mediastinum, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the anterior dissection, carefully manipulating the tissue adjacent to the crura and extending the opening of the peritoneal covering.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac from the left cruce, ensuring separation from the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in visualizing the hernia sac, attempting to reduce it completely within the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the camera to a thirty-degree upward angle, enhancing visualization of the hernia sac and identifying the precise area for dissection to pull the sac down.
The hernia sac is nearly completely reduced, with only a small portion remaining visible as the surgeon continues the dissection.
The surgeon creates a posterior window around the distal esophagus, adjusting the approach from outside the crura to facilitate the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a Penrose retractor to manage the stomach, ensuring it is not obstructing the surgical field during the hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a Penrose retractor to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring it extends beyond the crura, indicating sufficient length for the procedure.
The camera is positioned high in the mediastinum with a thirty-degree upward angle, providing a clear view of the posterior plane between the thin, splayed esophagus and the aorta.
The surgeon continues dissection in the plane between the esophagus and the aorta, further reducing the hernia sac.
The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection around the esophagus, aiming to achieve sufficient intra-abdominal length for the procedure.
The surgeon prepares for a high dissection in the mediastinum to achieve sufficient intra-abdominal length of the esophagus, ensuring it is mobilized adequately.
The surgeon advances the camera deep into the mediastinum, enhancing the visualization necessary for the full circumferential mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon has achieved sufficient intra-abdominal length of the esophagus and begins closing the crura, with visible manipulation of the tissue around the esophageal hiatus.

The surgeon closes the crura using a V-Lock suture, addressing the widely spaced crura with increased tension by placing multiple sutures before tightening, similar to techniques used in fascial repair during ventral hernia repair.
The surgeon re-approximates the cruse along the posterior edge, revealing a large lateral space that needs closure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to begin suturing from the right lateral position, aiming to approximate the widely spaced cruse.
The surgeon adjusts the sutures to relieve tension during the posterior closure of the cruse, ensuring a more secure approximation of the tissue.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to re-approximate the cruse to the esophagus, visibly bringing the structures together with precision.
The surgeon places an additional suture in the anterior space of the cruse, addressing the large gap visible in the surgical field.
